revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''土御門流'''（つちみかどりゅう）とは、[[村上源氏]][[源師房]]（土御門右大臣）を祖とする[[有職故実]]の流派のこと。...'
revision.text:
'''土御門流'''（つちみかどりゅう）とは、[[村上源氏]][[源師房]]（土御門右大臣）を祖とする[[有職故実]]の流派のこと。
師房は[[具平親王]]の子として生まれたが幼くして父を失い、[[臣籍降下]]して姉婿である[[藤原頼通]]の[[猶子]]（事実上の養子）として育てられた。そのため、[[藤原北家][[摂関家]]に伝えられてきた[[九条流]]及び[[御堂流]]の有職故実を身につけるとともに、自己の日記である『[[土右記]]』や著書である『[[叙位除目抄]]』に有職故実のついての知識や自説を書き記した。その後、息子の[[源俊房]]やその子供にあたる[[源師頼|師頼]]・[[源師時|師時]]にその知識は継承され、更に必要に応じて父祖の説への追加・修正を施された。このようにして継承された村上源氏の有職故実を「土御門流」を称されて重んじられた。
師房－俊房の系統は[[永久の変]]によって衰退するものの、俊房の弟である[[源顕房]]の系統が村上源氏の嫡流として栄え、[[源雅実]]らによって土御門流の学説を継承したため、世間から重んじられた。
{{工事中}}

'''土御門流'''（つちみかどりゅう）とは、[[村上源氏]][[源師房]]（土御門右大臣）を祖とする[[有職故実]]の流派のこと。
師房は[[具平親王]]の子として生まれたが幼くして父を失い、[[臣籍降下]]して姉婿である[[藤原頼通]]の[[猶子]]（事実上の養子）として育てられた。そのため、[[藤原北家]][[摂関家]]に伝えられてきた[[九条流]]及び[[御堂流]]の有職故実を身につけるとともに、自己の日記である『[[土右記]]』や著書である『[[叙位除目抄]]』に有職故実のついての知識や自説を書き記した。その後、息子の[[源俊房]]やその子供にあたる[[源師頼|師頼]]・[[源師時|師時]]にその知識は継承され、更に必要に応じて父祖の説への追加・修正を施された。このようにして継承された村上源氏の有職故実を「土御門流」を称されて重んじられた。
師房－俊房の系統は[[永久の変]]によって衰退するものの、俊房の弟である[[源顕房]]の系統が村上源氏の嫡流として栄え、[[源雅実]]らによって土御門流の学説を継承したため、世間から重んじられた。
{{工事中}}

'''土御門流'''（つちみかどりゅう）とは、[[村上源氏]][[源師房]]（土御門右大臣）を祖とする[[有職故実]]の流派のこと。
師房は[[具平親王]]の子として生まれたが幼くして父を失い、[[臣籍降下]]して姉婿である[[藤原頼通]]の[[猶子]]（事実上の養子）として育てられた。そのため、[[藤原北家]][[摂関家]]に伝えられてきた[[九条流]]及び[[御堂流]]の有職故実を身につけるとともに、自己の日記である『[[土右記]]』や著書である『[[叙位除目抄]]』に有職故実のついての知識や自説を書き記した。その後、息子の[[源俊房]]やその子供にあたる[[源師頼|師頼]]・[[源師時|師時]]にその知識は日記・書物・口伝の形で継承され、更に必要に応じて父祖の説への追加・修正を施された。このようにして継承された村上源氏の有職故実を「土御門流」を称されて重んじられた。
師房－俊房の系統は[[永久の変]]によって衰退するものの、俊房の弟である[[源顕房]]の系統が村上源氏の嫡流として栄え、[[源雅実]]らによって土御門流の学説を継承したため、世間から重んじられた。
源師頼を「先師」（『[[台記]]』仁平元年2月23日条・久寿元年1月23日条）、自身を「弟子」（同康治2年7月23日条）と称していた[[藤原頼長]]は、[[漢学]]のみならず[[公事]]や有職故実の[[先例]]も師頼に学び、土御門流の先例を求めて『土右記』などをたびたび参照していたことが知られている。また、[[後三条源氏]][[源有仁]]も母方が村上源氏の出身（俊房・顕房の弟[[源師忠|師忠]]の娘）であることから、土御門流を学び更に自説を加えて[[花園流]]（[[花園説]]）とした。後三条源氏は有仁1代で断絶するものの、その学説・文書は縁戚である[[閑院流]]に継承され、村上源氏の土御門流・閑院流の花園流が非摂関家諸家における有職故実の流派として重んじられ、御堂流を奉じる摂関家とは公事の有り方を巡ってたびたび論争を行った。
== 参考文献 == *細谷勘資「平安時代後期の礼儀作法と村上源氏」（初出:十世紀研究会 編『中世成立期の歴史像』（東京堂出版、1993年） ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3/改題「村上源氏の台頭と儀式作法の成立」:細谷勘資氏遺稿集刊行会 編『中世宮廷儀式書成立史の研究』（勉誠出版、2007年） ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1）
== 関連項目 == *[[村上源氏]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:つちみかとりゆう}} [[Category:平安時代]]

'''土御門流'''（つちみかどりゅう）とは、[[村上源氏]][[源師房]]（土御門右大臣）を祖とする[[有職故実]]の流派のこと。[[源師頼|師頼]]・[[源師時|師時]]兄弟によって体系化されたと言われている。
師房は[[具平親王]]の子として生まれたが幼くして父を失い、[[臣籍降下]]して姉婿である[[藤原頼通]]の[[猶子]]（事実上の養子）として育てられた。そのため、[[藤原北家]][[摂関家]]に伝えられてきた[[九条流]]及び[[御堂流]]の有職故実を身につけるとともに、自己の日記である『[[土右記]]』や著書である『[[叙位除目抄]]』に有職故実のついての知識や自説を書き記した。その後、息子の[[源俊房]]やその子供にあたる師頼・師時兄弟にその知識は日記・書物・口伝の形で継承され、更に必要に応じて父祖の説への追加・修正を施されていった。このようにして継承された村上源氏の有職故実を「土御門流」を称されて重んじられた。
師房－俊房の系統は[[永久の変]]によって衰退するものの、俊房の弟である[[源顕房]]の系統が村上源氏の嫡流として栄え、[[源雅実]]らによって土御門流の学説を継承したため、世間から重んじられた。

== 参考文献 == *細谷勘資「平安時代後期の礼儀作法と村上源氏」（初出:十世紀研究会 編『中世成立期の歴史像』（東京堂出版、1993年） ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3/改題「村上源氏の台頭と儀式作法の成立」:細谷勘資氏遺稿集刊行会 編『中世宮廷儀式書成立史の研究』（勉誠出版、2007年） ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1）

'''土御門流'''（つちみかどりゅう）とは、[[村上源氏]]中院流[[源師房]]（土御門右大臣）を祖とする[[有職故実]]の流派のこと。師房の孫である[[源師頼|師頼]]・[[源師時|師時]]兄弟によって体系化されたと言われている。

== 概要 == 師房は[[具平親王]]の子として生まれたが幼くして父を失い、[[臣籍降下]]して姉婿である[[藤原頼通]]の[[猶子]]（事実上の養子）として育てられた。そのため、[[藤原北家]][[摂関家]]に伝えられてきた[[九条流]]及び[[御堂流]]の有職故実を身につけるとともに、自己の日記である『[[土右記]]』や著書である『[[叙位除目抄]]』に有職故実のついての知識や自説を書き記した。その後、息子の[[源俊房]]やその子供にあたる師頼・師時兄弟にその知識は日記・書物・口伝の形で継承され、更に必要に応じて父祖の説への追加・修正を施されていった。このようにして継承された村上源氏中院流の有職故実を「土御門流」を称されて重んじられた。
師房－俊房の系統は[[永久の変]]によって衰退するものの、俊房の弟である[[源顕房]]の系統が中院流の[[嫡流]]として栄え、[[源雅実]]らによって土御門流の学説を継承したため、世間から重んじられた。
源師頼を「先師」（『[[台記]]』仁平元年2月23日条・久寿元年1月23日条）、自身を「弟子」（同康治2年7月23日条）と称していた[[藤原頼長]]は、[[漢学]]のみならず[[公事]]や有職故実の[[先例]]も師頼に学び、土御門流の先例を求めて『土右記』などをたびたび参照していたことが知られている。また、[[源氏#後三条源氏|後三条源氏]][[源有仁]]も母方が村上源氏の出身（俊房・顕房の弟[[源師忠|師忠]]の娘）であることから、土御門流を学び更に自説を加えて[[花園流]]（[[花園説]]）とした。後三条源氏は有仁1代で断絶するものの、その学説・文書は縁戚である[[閑院流]]に継承され、村上源氏中院流の土御門流・閑院流の花園流が非[[摂家|摂関家]]諸家における有職故実の流派として重んじられ、御堂流を奉じる摂関家とは公事の有り方を巡ってたびたび論争を行った。
== 参考文献 == * 細谷勘資「平安時代後期の礼儀作法と村上源氏」（初出:十世紀研究会 編『中世成立期の歴史像』（[[東京堂出版]]、[[1993年]]） ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3/改題「村上源氏の台頭と儀式作法の成立」:細谷勘資氏遺稿集刊行会 編『中世宮廷儀式書成立史の研究』（[[勉誠出版]]、[[2007年]]） ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1）
== 関連項目 == * [[村上源氏]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:つちみかとりゆう}} [[Category:平安時代]] [[Category:村上源氏]]

revision.text:
'''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流)
The '''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流, Tsuchimikado-ryū) refers to a school of {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実}} (court practices and precedents) originating from the {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}} Chūin branch, founded by {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房}}, also known as the "Tsuchimikado Udaijin" (土御門右大臣). This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時}}.
== Overview ==
Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the {{ill|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)|ja|藤原頼通}} household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流}} of the {{ill|Fujiwara Northern House|ja|藤原北家}}, alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|ja|藤原頼長}}, who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).
== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}}
[[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:Murakami Genji]] [[ja:土御門流]]

'''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流)
The '''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流, Tsuchimikado-ryū) refers to a school of {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実}} (court practices and precedents) originating from the {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}} Chūin branch, founded by {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房}}, also known as the "Tsuchimikado Udaijin" (土御門右大臣). This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時}}.
== Overview ==
Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the {{ill|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)|ja|藤原頼通}} household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流}} of the {{ill|Fujiwara Northern House|ja|藤原北家}}, alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|ja|藤原頼長}}, who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).
== Related Articles ==

revision.text:
'''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流)
The '''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流, Tsuchimikado-ryū) refers to a school of {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実}} (court practices and precedents) originating from the {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}} Chūin branch, founded by {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房}}, also known as the "Tsuchimikado Udaijin" (土御門右大臣). This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時}}.
== Overview ==
Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the {{ill|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)|ja|藤原頼通}} household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流}} of the {{ill|Fujiwara Northern House|ja|藤原北家}}, alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|ja|藤原頼長}}, who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).
== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}}

'''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流)
The '''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流, Tsuchimikado-ryū) refers to a school of {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実}} (court practices and precedents) originating from the {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}} Chūin branch, founded by {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房}}, also known as the "Tsuchimikado Udaijin" (土御門右大臣). This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時}}.
== Overview ==
Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the {{ill|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)|ja|藤原頼通}} household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流}} of the {{ill|Fujiwara Northern House|ja|藤原北家}}, alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|ja|藤原頼長}}, who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).
== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}}

revision.text:
'''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流)
The '''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流, Tsuchimikado-ryū) refers to a school of {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実}} (court practices and precedents) originating from the {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}} Chūin branch, founded by {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房}}, also known as the "Tsuchimikado Udaijin" (土御門右大臣). This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時}}.
== Overview ==
Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the {{ill|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)|ja|藤原頼通}} household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流}} of the {{ill|Fujiwara Northern House|ja|藤原北家}}, alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|ja|藤原頼長}}, who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).
== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}}

His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|ja|藤原頼長}}, who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).

These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.

The '''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流, Tsuchimikado-ryū) refers to a school of {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実}} (court practices and precedents) originating from the {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}} Chūin branch, founded by {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房}}, also known as the "Tsuchimikado Udaijin" (土御門右大臣). This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時}}.
== Overview ==
Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the {{ill|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)|ja|藤原頼通}} household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流}} of the {{ill|Fujiwara Northern House|ja|藤原北家}}, alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|ja|藤原頼長}}, who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==

The '''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流, Tsuchimikado-ryū) refers to a school of {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実}} (court practices and precedents) originating from the {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}} Chūin branch, founded by {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房}}, also known as the "Tsuchimikado Udaijin" (土御門右大臣). This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時}}.
== Overview ==
Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the {{ill|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)|ja|藤原頼通}} household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流}} of the {{ill|Fujiwara Northern House|ja|藤原北家}}, alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|ja|藤原頼長}}, who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).

The '''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流, Tsuchimikado-ryū) refers to a school of {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実}} (court practices and precedents) originating from the {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}} Chūin branch, founded by {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房}}, also known as the "Tsuchimikado Udaijin" (土御門右大臣). This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時}}.== Overview ==Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the {{ill|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)|ja|藤原頼通}} household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流}} of the {{ill|Fujiwara Northern House|ja|藤原北家}}, alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|ja|藤原頼長}}, who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. The '''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流, Tsuchimikado-ryū) refers to a school of {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実}} (court practices and precedents) originating from the {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}} Chūin branch, founded by {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房}}, also known as the "Tsuchimikado Udaijin" (土御門右大臣). This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時}}.== Overview ==Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the {{ill|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)|ja|藤原頼通}} household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流}} of the {{ill|Fujiwara Northern House|ja|藤原北家}}, alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|ja|藤原頼長}}, who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.== References ==* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).== Related Articles ==* {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}}]][[Category:Heian period]]]][[Category:Murakami Genji]]]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tsuchimikado school-qq]] ]][[Category:Tsuchimikado school]]]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tsuchimikado school-qq]] ]][[Category:Tsuchimikado school]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Tsuchimikado school}}]][[Category:pp]]]][[category:Tsuchimikado school]]]][[Category:pp]]]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tsuchimikado school-qq]] ]][[Category:Tsuchimikado school]]]][[category:Tsuchimikado school]]]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tsuchimikado school-qq]] ]][[Category:Tsuchimikado school]]]][[Category:pp]]]][[category:Tsuchimikado school]]]][[Category:pp]]]][[Category:pp]]]][[category:Tsuchimikado school]]]][[Category:pp]]]][[Category:Tsuchimikado school]]]][[Category:mass bump]]]][[Category:1 revision imported]]]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]]]][[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]]]][[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Tsuchimikado school]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsuchimikado school]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Tsuchimikado school]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsuchimikado school]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsuchimikado school]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsuchimikado school]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsuchimikado school]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Tsuchimikado school]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Tsuchimikado school]][[Category:11]] ]][[ja:土御門流]]

The '''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流, Tsuchimikado-ryū) refers to a school of {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実}} (court practices and precedents) originating from the {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}} Chūin branch, founded by {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房}}, also known as the "Tsuchimikado Udaijin" (土御門右大臣). This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時}}.== Overview ==Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the {{ill|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)|ja|藤原頼通}} household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流}} of the {{ill|Fujiwara Northern House|ja|藤原北家}}, alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|ja|藤原頼長}}, who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.== References ==* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).== Related Articles ==* {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}}]][[Category:Heian period]]]][[Category:Murakami Genji]]]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tsuchimikado school-qq]] ]][[Category:Tsuchimikado school]]]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tsuchimikado school-qq]] ]][[Category:Tsuchimikado school]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Tsuchimikado school}}]][[Category:pp]]]][[category:Tsuchimikado school]]]][[Category:pp]]]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tsuchimikado school-qq]] ]][[Category:Tsuchimikado school]]]][[category:Tsuchimikado school]]]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tsuchimikado school-qq]] ]][[Category:Tsuchimikado school]]]][[Category:pp]]]][[category:Tsuchimikado school]]]][[Category:pp]]]][[Category:pp]]]][[category:Tsuchimikado school]]]][[Category:pp]]]][[Category:Tsuchimikado school]]]][[Category:mass bump]]]][[Category:1 revision imported]]]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]]]][[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]]]][[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]][[sn:Tsuchimikado school]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsuchimikado school]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]][[sn:Tsuchimikado school]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsuchimikado school]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsuchimikado school]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsuchimikado school]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsuchimikado school]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Tsuchimikado school]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Tsuchimikado school]][[Category:11]] ]][[ja:土御門流]]

This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時}}.== Overview ==Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the {{ill|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)|ja|藤原頼通}} household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流}} of the {{ill|Fujiwara Northern House|ja|藤原北家}}, alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|ja|藤原頼長}}, who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.== References ==* Hosoya, Kansuke. The '''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流, Tsuchimikado-ryū) refers to a school of {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実}} (court practices and precedents) originating from the {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}} Chūin branch, founded by {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房}}, also known as the "Tsuchimikado Udaijin" (土御門右大臣). This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時}}.
== Overview ==
Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the {{ill|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)|ja|藤原頼通}} household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流}} of the {{ill|Fujiwara Northern House|ja|藤原北家}}, alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|ja|藤原頼長}}, who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).

For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|ja|藤原頼長}}, who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.

The '''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流, Tsuchimikado-ryū) refers to a school of {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実}} (court practices and precedents) originating from the {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}} Chūin branch, founded by {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房}}, also known as the "Tsuchimikado Udaijin" (土御門右大臣). This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時}}.
== Overview ==
Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the {{ill|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)|ja|藤原頼通}} household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流}} of the {{ill|Fujiwara Northern House|ja|藤原北家}}, alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|ja|藤原頼長}}, who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).

[[ja:土御門流]]

The '''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流, Tsuchimikado-ryū) refers to a school of {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実}} (court practices and precedents) originating from the {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}} Chūin branch, founded by {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房}}, also known as the "Tsuchimikado Udaijin" (土御門右大臣). This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時}}.
== Overview ==
Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the {{ill|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)|ja|藤原頼通}} household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流}} of the {{ill|Fujiwara Northern House|ja|藤原北家}}, alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|ja|藤原頼長}}, who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.

[[ja:土御門流]] [[Category:New]]

The '''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流, Tsuchimikado-ryū) refers to a school of {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実}} (court practices and precedents) originating from the {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}} Chūin branch, founded by {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房}}, also known as the "Tsuchimikado Udaijin" (土御門右大臣). This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時}}.
== Overview ==
Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the {{ill|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)|ja|藤原頼通}} household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流}} of the {{ill|Fujiwara Northern House|ja|藤原北家}}, alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|ja|藤原頼長}}, who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shu11an, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).
== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}}

Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. The '''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流, Tsuchimikado-ryū) refers to a school of {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実}} (court practices and precedents) originating from the {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}} Chūin branch, founded by {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房}}, also known as the "Tsuchimikado Udaijin" (土御門右大臣). This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時}}.
== Overview ==
Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the {{ill|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)|ja|藤原頼通}} household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流}} of the {{ill|Fujiwara Northern House|ja|藤原北家}}, alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|ja|藤原頼長}}, who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shu11an, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).
== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}}

8 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:土御門流 up to rev 75436981
revision.text:
'''土御門流'''（つちみかどりゅう）とは、[[村上源氏]]中院流[[源師房]]（土御門右大臣）を祖とする[[有職故実]]の流派のこと。師房の孫である[[源師頼|師頼]]・[[源師時|師時]]兄弟によって体系化されたと言われている。
== 概要 == 師房は[[具平親王]]の子として生まれたが幼くして父を失い、[[臣籍降下]]して姉婿である[[藤原頼通]]の[[猶子]]（事実上の養子）として育てられた。そのため、[[藤原北家]][[摂関家]]に伝えられてきた[[九条流]]及び[[御堂流]]の有職故実を身につけるとともに、自己の日記である『[[土右記]]』や著書である『[[叙位除目抄]]』に有職故実のついての知識や自説を書き記した。その後、息子の[[源俊房]]やその子供にあたる師頼・師時兄弟にその知識は日記・書物・口伝の形で継承され、更に必要に応じて父祖の説への追加・修正を施されていった。このようにして継承された村上源氏中院流の有職故実を「土御門流」を称されて重んじられた。
師房－俊房の系統は[[永久の変]]によって衰退するものの、俊房の弟である[[源顕房]]の系統が中院流の[[嫡流]]として栄え、[[源雅実]]らによって土御門流の学説を継承したため、世間から重んじられた。
源師頼を「先師」（『[[台記]]』仁平元年2月23日条・久寿元年1月23日条）、自身を「弟子」（同康治2年7月23日条）と称していた[[藤原頼長]]は、[[漢学]]のみならず[[公事]]や有職故実の[[先例]]も師頼に学び、土御門流の先例を求めて『土右記』などをたびたび参照していたことが知られている。また、[[源氏#後三条源氏|後三条源氏]][[源有仁]]も母方が村上源氏の出身（俊房・顕房の弟[[源師忠|師忠]]の娘）であることから、土御門流を学び更に自説を加えて[[花園流]]（[[花園説]]）とした。後三条源氏は有仁1代で断絶するものの、その学説・文書は縁戚である[[閑院流]]に継承され、村上源氏中院流の土御門流・閑院流の花園流が非[[摂家|摂関家]]諸家における有職故実の流派として重んじられ、御堂流を奉じる摂関家とは公事の有り方を巡ってたびたび論争を行った。
== 参考文献 == * 細谷勘資「平安時代後期の礼儀作法と村上源氏」（初出:十世紀研究会 編『中世成立期の歴史像』（[[東京堂出版]]、[[1993年]]） ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3/改題「村上源氏の台頭と儀式作法の成立」:細谷勘資氏遺稿集刊行会 編『中世宮廷儀式書成立史の研究』（[[勉誠出版]]、[[2007年]]） ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1）
== 関連項目 == * [[村上源氏]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:つちみかとりゆう}} [[Category:平安時代]] [[Category:村上源氏]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[土御門流]] to [[Tsuchimikado school]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
'''土御門流'''（つちみかどりゅう）とは、[[村上源氏]]中院流[[源師房]]（土御門右大臣）を祖とする[[有職故実]]の流派のこと。師房の孫である[[源師頼|師頼]]・[[源師時|師時]]兄弟によって体系化されたと言われている。
== 概要 == 師房は[[具平親王]]の子として生まれたが幼くして父を失い、[[臣籍降下]]して姉婿である[[藤原頼通]]の[[猶子]]（事実上の養子）として育てられた。そのため、[[藤原北家]][[摂関家]]に伝えられてきた[[九条流]]及び[[御堂流]]の有職故実を身につけるとともに、自己の日記である『[[土右記]]』や著書である『[[叙位除目抄]]』に有職故実のついての知識や自説を書き記した。その後、息子の[[源俊房]]やその子供にあたる師頼・師時兄弟にその知識は日記・書物・口伝の形で継承され、更に必要に応じて父祖の説への追加・修正を施されていった。このようにして継承された村上源氏中院流の有職故実を「土御門流」を称されて重んじられた。
師房－俊房の系統は[[永久の変]]によって衰退するものの、俊房の弟である[[源顕房]]の系統が中院流の[[嫡流]]として栄え、[[源雅実]]らによって土御門流の学説を継承したため、世間から重んじられた。
源師頼を「先師」（『[[台記]]』仁平元年2月23日条・久寿元年1月23日条）、自身を「弟子」（同康治2年7月23日条）と称していた[[藤原頼長]]は、[[漢学]]のみならず[[公事]]や有職故実の[[先例]]も師頼に学び、土御門流の先例を求めて『土右記』などをたびたび参照していたことが知られている。また、[[源氏#後三条源氏|後三条源氏]][[源有仁]]も母方が村上源氏の出身（俊房・顕房の弟[[源師忠|師忠]]の娘）であることから、土御門流を学び更に自説を加えて[[花園流]]（[[花園説]]）とした。後三条源氏は有仁1代で断絶するものの、その学説・文書は縁戚である[[閑院流]]に継承され、村上源氏中院流の土御門流・閑院流の花園流が非[[摂家|摂関家]]諸家における有職故実の流派として重んじられ、御堂流を奉じる摂関家とは公事の有り方を巡ってたびたび論争を行った。
== 参考文献 == * 細谷勘資「平安時代後期の礼儀作法と村上源氏」（初出:十世紀研究会 編『中世成立期の歴史像』（[[東京堂出版]]、[[1993年]]） ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3/改題「村上源氏の台頭と儀式作法の成立」:細谷勘資氏遺稿集刊行会 編『中世宮廷儀式書成立史の研究』（[[勉誠出版]]、[[2007年]]） ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1）
== 関連項目 == * [[村上源氏]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:つちみかとりゆう}} [[Category:平安時代]] [[Category:村上源氏]]

The '''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流, Tsuchimikado-ryū) refers to a school of {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (court practices and precedents) originating from the {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Chūin branch, founded by {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, also known as the "Tsuchimikado Udaijin" (土御門右大臣). This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Overview ==
Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the {{ill|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)|ja|藤原頼通|qq=draft|qq=draft}} household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of the {{ill|Fujiwara Northern House|ja|藤原北家|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実|qq=draft|qq=draft}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|ja|藤原頼長|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shu11an, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).
== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Tsuchimikado school}} {{translated page|ja|土御門流|version=75436981|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.

This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujō School|qq=}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Midō School|qq=}} of the [[:en:Fujiwara Hok-ke|Fujiwara Northern House]], alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchiu-ki|qq=}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joi Jimyōshō|qq=}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Toshifusa|qq=}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eikyū Disturbance|qq=}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Akifusa|qq=}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Masazane|qq=}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorinaga|Fujiwara no Yorinaga]], who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Arihito|qq=}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Sanjō Genji|qq=}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono School|qq=}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanin School|qq=}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shu11an, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).

This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Moroyori|qq=}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Morotoki|qq=}}.
== Overview ==
Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Tomohira|qq=}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorimichi|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)]] household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujō School|qq=}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Midō School|qq=}} of the [[:en:Fujiwara Hok-ke|Fujiwara Northern House]], alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchiu-ki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joi Jimyōshō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Toshifusa|qq=}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eikyū Disturbance|qq=}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Akifusa|qq=}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Masazane|qq=}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorinaga|Fujiwara no Yorinaga]], who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Arihito|qq=}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Sanjō Genji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 源氏}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono School|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanin School|qq=}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.

Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11423491]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} The '''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流, Tsuchimikado-ryū) refers to a school of {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku Kojitsu|qq=}} (court practices and precedents) originating from the {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakami Genji|qq=|1=Murakami-Genji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3867344}} Chūin branch, founded by {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Morofusa|qq=|1=Minamoto no Morofusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564069}}, also known as the "Tsuchimikado Udaijin" (土御門右大臣). This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Moroyori|qq=|1=Minamoto no Moroyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564072}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Morotoki|qq=|1=Minamoto no Morotoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564068}}.
== Overview ==
Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Tomohira|qq=|1=Tomohira-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393729}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorimichi|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)]] household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujō School|qq=|1=Kujo school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370406}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Midō School|qq=|1=Midō-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488901}} of the [[:en:Fujiwara Hok-ke|Fujiwara Northern House]], alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchiu-ki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joi Jimyōshō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Toshifusa|qq=|1=Minamoto no Toshifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563990}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eikyū Disturbance|qq=|1=Eikyū incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549672}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Akifusa|qq=|1=Minamoto no Akifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564369}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Masazane|qq=|1=Minamoto no Masazane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564335}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorinaga|Fujiwara no Yorinaga]], who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Arihito|qq=|1=Arihito Minamoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564123}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Sanjō Genji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 源氏|1=Minamoto clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q599881}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono School|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanin School|qq=|1=Kanin School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655354}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shu11an, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).

His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Toshifusa|qq=|1=Minamoto no Toshifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563990}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eikyū Disturbance|qq=|1=Eikyū incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549672}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Akifusa|qq=|1=Minamoto no Akifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564369}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Masazane|qq=|1=Minamoto no Masazane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564335}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
