This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujō School|qq=|1=Kujo school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370406}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Midō School|qq=|1=Midō-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488901}} of the [[:en:Fujiwara Hok-ke|Fujiwara Northern House]], alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchiu-ki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joi Jimyōshō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Toshifusa|qq=|1=Minamoto no Toshifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563990}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eikyū Disturbance|qq=|1=Eikyū incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549672}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Akifusa|qq=|1=Minamoto no Akifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564369}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Masazane|qq=|1=Minamoto no Masazane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564335}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorinaga|Fujiwara no Yorinaga]], who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Arihito|qq=|1=Arihito Minamoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564123}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Sanjō Genji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 源氏|1=Minamoto clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q599881}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono School|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanin School|qq=|1=Kanin School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655354}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shu11an, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).

This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Moroyori|qq=|1=Minamoto no Moroyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564072}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Morotoki|qq=|1=Minamoto no Morotoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564068}}.

His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Toshifusa|qq=|1=Minamoto no Toshifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563990}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eikyū Disturbance|qq=|1=Eikyū incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549672}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Akifusa|qq=|1=Minamoto no Akifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564369}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Masazane|qq=|1=Minamoto no Masazane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564335}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.

Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Arihito|qq=|1=Arihito Minamoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564123}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Sanjō Genji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 源氏|1=Minamoto clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q599881}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono School|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Moroyori|qq=|1=Minamoto no Moroyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564072}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Morotoki|qq=|1=Minamoto no Morotoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564068}}.
== Overview ==
Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Tomohira|qq=|1=Tomohira-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393729}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorimichi|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)]] household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujō School|qq=|1=Kujo school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370406}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Midō School|qq=|1=Midō-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488901}} of the [[:en:Fujiwara Hok-ke|Fujiwara Northern House]], alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchiu-ki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joi Jimyōshō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Toshifusa|qq=|1=Minamoto no Toshifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563990}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eikyū Disturbance|qq=|1=Eikyū incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549672}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Akifusa|qq=|1=Minamoto no Akifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564369}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Masazane|qq=|1=Minamoto no Masazane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564335}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorinaga|Fujiwara no Yorinaga]], who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Arihito|qq=|1=Arihito Minamoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564123}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Sanjō Genji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 源氏|1=Minamoto clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q599881}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono School|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanin School|qq=|1=Kanin School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655354}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.

The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorinaga|Fujiwara no Yorinaga]], who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Arihito|1=Arihito Minamoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564123}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Sanjō Genji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 源氏|1=Minamoto clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q599881}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanin School|1=Kanin School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655354}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shu11an, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).

This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Moroyori|1=Minamoto no Moroyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564072}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Morotoki|1=Minamoto no Morotoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564068}}.

Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Tomohira|1=Tomohira-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393729}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorimichi|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)]] household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujō School|1=Kujo school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370406}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Midō School|1=Midō-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488901}} of the [[:en:Fujiwara Hok-ke|Fujiwara Northern House]], alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchiu-ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joi Jimyōshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Toshifusa|1=Minamoto no Toshifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563990}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eikyū Disturbance|1=Eikyū incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549672}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Akifusa|1=Minamoto no Akifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564369}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Masazane|1=Minamoto no Masazane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564335}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorinaga|Fujiwara no Yorinaga]], who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Arihito|1=Arihito Minamoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564123}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Sanjō Genji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 源氏|1=Minamoto clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q599881}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanin School|1=Kanin School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655354}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.

His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Toshifusa|1=Minamoto no Toshifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563990}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eikyū Disturbance|1=Eikyū incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549672}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Akifusa|1=Minamoto no Akifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564369}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Masazane|1=Minamoto no Masazane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564335}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorinaga|Fujiwara no Yorinaga]], who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Arihito|1=Arihito Minamoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564123}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Sanjō Genji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 源氏|1=Minamoto clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q599881}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanin School|1=Kanin School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655354}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.

This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Moroyori|1=Minamoto no Moroyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564072}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Morotoki|1=Minamoto no Morotoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564068}}.
== Overview ==
Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Tomohira|1=Tomohira-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393729}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorimichi|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)]] household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujō School|1=Kujo school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370406}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Midō School|1=Midō-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488901}} of the [[:en:Fujiwara Hok-ke|Fujiwara Northern House]], alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchiu-ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joi Jimyōshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Toshifusa|1=Minamoto no Toshifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563990}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eikyū Disturbance|1=Eikyū incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549672}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Akifusa|1=Minamoto no Akifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564369}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Masazane|1=Minamoto no Masazane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564335}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorinaga|Fujiwara no Yorinaga]], who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Arihito|1=Arihito Minamoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564123}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Sanjō Genji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 源氏|1=Minamoto clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q599881}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanin School|1=Kanin School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655354}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shu11an, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).
== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakami Genji|1=Murakami-Genji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3867344}}

Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanin School|1=Kanin School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655354}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shu11an, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:土御門流]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11423491]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} The '''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流, Tsuchimikado-ryū) refers to a school of {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku Kojitsu|qq=}} (court practices and precedents) originating from the {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakami Genji|1=Murakami-Genji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3867344}} Chūin branch, founded by {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Morofusa|1=Minamoto no Morofusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564069}}, also known as the "Tsuchimikado Udaijin" (土御門右大臣). This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Moroyori|1=Minamoto no Moroyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564072}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Morotoki|1=Minamoto no Morotoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564068}}.
== Overview ==
Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Tomohira|1=Tomohira-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393729}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorimichi|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)]] household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujō School|1=Kujo school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370406}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Midō School|1=Midō-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488901}} of the [[:en:Fujiwara Hok-ke|Fujiwara Northern House]], alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchiu-ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joi Jimyōshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Toshifusa|1=Minamoto no Toshifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563990}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eikyū Disturbance|1=Eikyū incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549672}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Akifusa|1=Minamoto no Akifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564369}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Masazane|1=Minamoto no Masazane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564335}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorinaga|Fujiwara no Yorinaga]], who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Arihito|1=Arihito Minamoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564123}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Sanjō Genji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 源氏|1=Minamoto clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q599881}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanin School|1=Kanin School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655354}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shu11an, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).
== Related Articles ==

His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Toshifusa|1=Minamoto no Toshifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563990}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eikyū Disturbance|1=Eikyū incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549672}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Akifusa|1=Minamoto no Akifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564369}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Masazane|1=Minamoto no Masazane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564335}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorinaga|Fujiwara no Yorinaga]], who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Arihito|1=Arihito Minamoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564123}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Sanjō Genji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 源氏|1=Minamoto clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q599881}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanin School|1=Kanin School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655354}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shu11an, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:土御門流]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:土御門流]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:土御門流]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11423491]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} The '''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流, Tsuchimikado-ryū) refers to a school of {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku Kojitsu|qq=}} (court practices and precedents) originating from the {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakami Genji|1=Murakami-Genji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3867344}} Chūin branch, founded by {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Morofusa|1=Minamoto no Morofusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564069}}, also known as the "Tsuchimikado Udaijin" (土御門右大臣). This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Moroyori|1=Minamoto no Moroyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564072}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Morotoki|1=Minamoto no Morotoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564068}}.
== Overview ==
Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Tomohira|1=Tomohira-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393729}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorimichi|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)]] household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujō School|1=Kujo school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370406}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Midō School|1=Midō-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488901}} of the [[:en:Fujiwara Hok-ke|Fujiwara Northern House]], alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchiu-ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joi Jimyōshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Toshifusa|1=Minamoto no Toshifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563990}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eikyū Disturbance|1=Eikyū incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549672}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Akifusa|1=Minamoto no Akifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564369}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Masazane|1=Minamoto no Masazane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564335}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorinaga|Fujiwara no Yorinaga]], who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Arihito|1=Arihito Minamoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564123}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Sanjō Genji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 源氏|1=Minamoto clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q599881}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanin School|1=Kanin School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655354}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shu11an, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).
== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakami Genji|1=Murakami-Genji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3867344}}
{{translated page|ja|土御門流|version=75436981|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Masazane|1=Minamoto no Masazane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564335}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.

Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:土御門流]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:土御門流]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:土御門流]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11423491]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} The '''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流, Tsuchimikado-ryū) refers to a school of {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku Kojitsu|qq=}} (court practices and precedents) originating from the {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakami Genji|1=Murakami-Genji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3867344}} Chūin branch, founded by {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Morofusa|1=Minamoto no Morofusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564069}}, also known as the "Tsuchimikado Udaijin" (土御門右大臣). This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Moroyori|1=Minamoto no Moroyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564072}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Morotoki|1=Minamoto no Morotoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564068}}.
== Overview ==
Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Tomohira|1=Tomohira-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393729}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorimichi|Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)]] household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujō School|1=Kujo school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370406}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Midō School|1=Midō-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488901}} of the [[:en:Fujiwara Hok-ke|Fujiwara Northern House]], alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchiu-ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joi Jimyōshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Toshifusa|1=Minamoto no Toshifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563990}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eikyū Disturbance|1=Eikyū incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549672}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Akifusa|1=Minamoto no Akifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564369}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Masazane|1=Minamoto no Masazane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564335}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorinaga|Fujiwara no Yorinaga]], who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Arihito|1=Arihito Minamoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564123}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Sanjō Genji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 源氏|1=Minamoto clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q599881}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanin School|1=Kanin School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655354}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shu11an, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).

Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Arihito|1=Arihito Minamoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564123}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Sanjō Genji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 源氏|1=Minamoto clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q599881}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanin School|1=Kanin School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655354}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
== References ==
* Hosoya, Kansuke. "Ceremonial Practices of the Heian Period and the Murakami Genji." First published in ''Chūsei Seiritsuki no Rekishizō'' (Tokyo-dō Shu11an, 1993, ISBN 978-4-490-20211-3). Republished as "The Rise of the Murakami Genji and the Formation of Ceremonial Practices" in ''Studies on the History of Medieval Court Ceremony Manuscripts'' (Bensei Publishing, 2007, ISBN 978-4-585-03158-1).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:土御門流]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:土御門流]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:土御門流]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11423491]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} The '''Tsuchimikado School''' (土御門流, Tsuchimikado-ryū) refers to a school of {{ill|Yūsoku Kojitsu|ja|有職故実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku Kojitsu|qq=}} (court practices and precedents) originating from the {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakami Genji|1=Murakami-Genji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3867344}} Chūin branch, founded by {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Morofusa|1=Minamoto no Morofusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564069}}, also known as the "Tsuchimikado Udaijin" (土御門右大臣). This system was later formalized by Morofusa's grandsons, {{ill|Minamoto no Moroyori|ja|源師頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Moroyori|1=Minamoto no Moroyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564072}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Morotoki|ja|源師時|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Morotoki|1=Minamoto no Morotoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564068}}.
== Overview ==
Morofusa, a son of {{ill|Prince Tomohira|ja|具平親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Tomohira|1=Tomohira-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393729}}, was orphaned at a young age and entered the {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorimichi|en|Fujiwara no Yorimichi|WD=Q1370620|lt=Minamoto no Makoto (court noble)}} household as a foster son. This upbringing allowed him to inherit the traditions of the {{ill|Kujō School|ja|九条流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujō School|1=Kujo school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370406}} and {{ill|Midō School|ja|御堂流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Midō School|1=Midō-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488901}} of the {{ill|Fujiwara Hok-ke|en|Fujiwara Hok-ke|WD=Q2975268|lt=Fujiwara Northern House}}, alongside his unique contributions written in his diary, ''{{ill|Tsuchiu-ki|ja|土右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchiu-ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', and his work, ''{{ill|Joi Jimyōshō|ja|叙位除目抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joi Jimyōshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''. His knowledge was passed down to his son, {{ill|Minamoto no Toshifusa|ja|源俊房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Toshifusa|1=Minamoto no Toshifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563990}}, and subsequently to Moroyori and Morotoki in the form of diaries, manuscripts, and oral traditions, with further enhancements and refinements.
Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eikyū Disturbance|1=Eikyū incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549672}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Akifusa|1=Minamoto no Akifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564369}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Masazane|1=Minamoto no Masazane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564335}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|en|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|WD=Q718947|lt=Fujiwara no Yorinaga}}, who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Arihito|1=Arihito Minamoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564123}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Sanjō Genji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 源氏|1=Minamoto clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q599881}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanin School|1=Kanin School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655354}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.

Although Morofusa’s lineage, represented by Toshifusa, suffered decline following the {{ill|Eikyū Disturbance|ja|永久の変|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eikyū Disturbance|1=Eikyū incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11549672}}, his younger brother, {{ill|Minamoto no Akifusa|ja|源顕房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Akifusa|1=Minamoto no Akifusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564369}}, continued the Chūin line, becoming the main inheritor of the Tsuchimikado traditions. Figures such as {{ill|Minamoto no Masazane|ja|源雅実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Masazane|1=Minamoto no Masazane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564335}} upheld and advanced the practices, earning recognition for the school.
The influence of the Tsuchimikado School extended beyond its family lineage. For example, {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|en|Fujiwara no Yorinaga|WD=Q718947|lt=Fujiwara no Yorinaga|1=Fujiwara no Yorinaga}}, who referred to Moroyori as "Master" (先師) and himself as a "disciple" (弟子), extensively studied court practices and precedents under Moroyori, frequently consulting texts such as the ''Tsuchiu-ki'' for guidance.
Additionally, {{ill|Minamoto no Arihito|ja|源有仁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Arihito|1=Arihito Minamoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564123}}, a descendant of the {{ill|Later Sanjō Genji|ja|後三条源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Sanjō Genji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 源氏|1=Minamoto clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q599881}}, who was maternally related to the Murakami Genji, studied the Tsuchimikado traditions and developed his own theories, later known as the {{ill|Hanazono School|ja|花園流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono School|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Despite the early extinction of Arihito’s lineage, the Hanazono School's theories and texts were inherited by the {{ill|Kanin School|ja|閑院流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanin School|1=Q11655354|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655354}}, contributing to the continuation and significance of both the Tsuchimikado and Hanazono traditions as prominent systems among non-regent families. These schools often engaged in debates with the Midō School, affiliated with the Fujiwara regent families, over ceremonial practices.
